title: Circular and Longitudinal Magnetization Techniques
narrationScript: This is the lesson where theory becomes a setup you can run. Every magnetization job comes down to two choices. Which direction must the flux flow, and how do we drive it there with electric current. The direction rule never changes. Flux must cross the discontinuity, ideally at a right angle, and anything between about forty five and ninety degrees still works. Since you rarely know flaw orientation in advance, full coverage means two shots, roughly perpendicular to each other. Circular magnetization puts current through the part itself, or through a conductor in its bore. Wrap your right hand around the current path with your thumb pointing with the current, and your fingers point along the flux. The field circles the current, so it crosses flaws that run parallel to the current. A head shot, with the part clamped between contact plates on a bench, finds seams and cracks running along a shaft. Current can also arrive through clamps and cables, through prods pressed against a weld, or by induction in ring shaped parts, where a changing flux through the ring drives current around it, and that current reveals circumferential cracks in races and rings. Remember the field profile from earlier in this module. Strongest at the outer surface of a solid bar, and essentially zero at the bore of a hollow part carrying its own current. For inside surfaces, run a central conductor through the bore. With the conductor resting against one side of the bore, count on good coverage over an arc of about four times the conductor diameter, then rotate the part and overlap each shot by about ten percent. For amperage, typical procedures call for three hundred to eight hundred amperes per inch of diameter on direct circular shots, and about ninety to one hundred twenty five amperes per inch of spacing for prods, with the prods set six to eight inches apart. Prods deserve respect. They concentrate current at two small contact points, so the hazards are arcing and overheating. Tips must be clean, contact must be firm before the current fires, and the circuit must be dead before the prods lift. An arc strike leaves a hard, cracked spot that can condemn a part, which is why many aerospace and finished surface specifications ban prods outright. Contact plates and headstocks obey the same physics, so they get copper braid pads, real clamping pressure, and contact area matched to the amperage. Every cable in the loop must be sized for the current, or it heats up and starves the shot. Longitudinal magnetization drives flux along the part instead of around it. Place the part inside a coil and the field runs down the axis, turning the part into a temporary magnet with poles at the ends. Now the flux crosses flaws that run across the part, transverse and circumferential cracks. Geometry rules the coil shot. The classic low fill factor rule says ampere turns equal forty five thousand divided by the length to diameter ratio, valid when that ratio falls between about two and fifteen. A part eighteen inches long and three inches in diameter has a ratio of six, so about seventy five hundred ampere turns, which a five turn coil delivers at about fifteen hundred amperes. Short chunky parts need pole pieces or stacking to raise the effective ratio. Long parts get magnetized in sections about eighteen inches at a time. Coils come as fixed bench windings, usually five turns a foot or so across, or as cable wraps you form on the spot for parts no bench can swallow. Choose the current type for the flaw you expect. Alternating current for surface service cracks, half wave or full wave direct current when subsurface matters. Then prove the field instead of trusting arithmetic. A shim with artificial flaws taped to the part confirms direction and strength together. A Hall effect gaussmeter should typically read about thirty to sixty gauss tangential at the surface where a measured value is required. The pie gauge gives a quick qualitative check of direction. And read the ammeter on every shot, because rectifiers drift and cables wear, and the meter reading is your record that the shot really happened at amperage. The yoke rounds out the kit. It is a portable electromagnet whose flux runs between the poles, finding cracks roughly perpendicular to the line between the legs. Verify it with a lifting check, about ten pounds for an alternating current yoke, about forty pounds for direct current, at the leg spacing you actually use. Two directions, calculated current, verified field. That is the whole craft of magnetization.
imagePrompt: An inspector in safety glasses presses two insulated-handle prods onto a dark structural steel weld while a light cloud of red dry magnetic powder drifts over the energized area in a fabrication shop.

sections.heading: Field Direction First
sections.body:
Magnetization by electric current is one decision wearing two costumes: **which way must the flux run**, and **what current arrangement puts it there**. The selection rule never changes — flux must cross the discontinuity at roughly **45–90°** for dependable leakage, and flux parallel to a flaw finds nothing. Because real flaw orientations are unknown, complete examination means **two magnetizations roughly perpendicular to each other**.

The right-hand rule converts current into flux direction instantly. Wrap your right hand around the current path, thumb with the current: fingers trace the flux circling it.

- **Current through the part (circular magnetization)** → flux circles the current → detects flaws **parallel to the current** (longitudinal seams in a shaft, flaws parallel to a prod line).
- **Current circling the part in a coil (longitudinal magnetization)** → flux runs along the part axis → detects flaws **across the part** (transverse and circumferential cracks).

Notice the pleasant symmetry: the flux is always perpendicular to the current that made it, so *flaws parallel to the current direction are the ones you find*. Keep that one sentence and you can derive every technique choice in this lesson.

sections.heading: Circular Techniques: Head Shots, Central Conductors, Induced Current
sections.body:
**Field in a part carrying current:** zero at the centerline of a solid bar, maximum at the surface, falling off outside as one over the distance — and essentially **zero at the bore of a hollow part** carrying its own current.

**Methods of putting current in the part:**

- **Head shot** — the part clamps between headstock contact plates on a bench unit; current flows end to end. Typical procedure values run **300–800 A per inch of diameter**.
- **Clamps and cables** — the same circuit improvised for parts that will not fit a bench.
- **Prods** — portable twin contacts for local shots on weldments and castings (next section).
- **Central conductor** — for hollow parts, a conductor through the bore carries the current; the **ID becomes the strongest-field surface**, with the OD also magnetized. An offset conductor resting against the bore wall gives reliable coverage over an arc of about **4× the conductor diameter**; rotate the part and overlap successive shots by about **10%**.
- **Induced current** — ring-shaped parts become the single-turn secondary of a transformer; the induced current flows around the ring and reveals **circumferential** cracks in bearing races and similar parts that have no good contact points.

**Depth factors:** current type still rules — AC stays in the skin; HWDC and FWDC push the usable field deeper for near-subsurface flaws.

**Discontinuities commonly detected:** seams, laps, and quench or grinding cracks running **parallel to the current path** — longitudinal flaws on shafts and tubes, ID cracks under a central conductor.

sections.caption: Head shot versus central conductor: direct current through a solid bar magnetizes its surface, while a central conductor through a hollow part puts the strongest field on the bore.

sections.heading: Prods and Contact Plates
sections.body:
**Prods** are hand-held contact electrodes that drive current through a local patch of a large part. The field circles the current path between the tips, so prods detect flaws **roughly parallel to the prod line** — you work the area twice, rotating the prod line about 90° between passes, advancing with overlap.

**Requirements for prods and plates:**

- Typical procedure values: **90–125 A per inch of prod spacing**, spacing commonly **6–8 in.** Closer spacing concentrates the field but raises burn risk; wider spacing weakens the field between tips. Use the low end of the amperage range on thin sections.
- Tips and pads must be **clean and dressed**; oxide, paint, and scale cause arcing. Contact areas on the part should be cleaned to bright metal where practical.
- A **remote-control switch on the prod handle** lets the operator make firm contact *before* energizing and break the circuit *before* lifting.
- Headstock **contact plates** follow the same physics at higher current: copper-braid pads to spread contact over real area, solid clamping pressure, and contact area matched to amperage — a part clamped lightly against a small pad will arc and burn just like a sloppy prod.

**Current-carrying capabilities:** every element — prods, pads, clamps, and the cables between them — must be sized for the shot current and duty cycle. Undersized or damaged cable heats, wastes voltage, and starves the shot; the ammeter shows the result.

sections.title: Arc strikes, burns, and overheating
sections.body: Never make or break contact while current flows. An arc at a prod tip or contact plate injects local heat that can leave a **hard, cracked, copper-contaminated spot** — an arc strike is itself a rejectable defect and can condemn an expensive part, which is why **many aerospace and finished-surface specifications prohibit prods entirely**. Watch part heating on repeated shots, especially thin sections and small contact areas; respect the equipment duty cycle; and keep flammable vapor away from contact arcing when using oil-carrier baths nearby.

sections.caption: Prod technique: the field circles the current path between the tips, so flaws parallel to the prod line are detected — typical values 90–125 A per inch of spacing at 6–8 in. spacing.

sections.heading: Longitudinal Technique: Coils and Cables
sections.body:
Pass current around the part instead of through it and you get **longitudinal magnetization** — the principle of the induced flux field. Each coil turn's circular field adds along the axis, so flux runs lengthwise through the part, which becomes a temporary bar magnet with **poles at its ends** (an external field you can feel with an indicator — unlike circular magnetization, which hides its flux inside the part).

**Geometry of the part** governs everything:

- The classic low fill-factor rule: **NI ≈ 45,000 ÷ (L/D)** ampere-turns, effective for **L/D between about 2 and 15**. Current direction is perpendicular to the resulting flux — fingers circle with the coil current, thumb points down the axis.
- **L/D below about 2:** the part self-demagnetizes strongly; stack parts end-to-end or add ferromagnetic pole pieces to raise the effective ratio.
- **Long parts:** the effective field zone of a coil is limited — magnetize in sections of roughly **18 in.**, moving the coil with overlap.

**Shapes, sizes, and current capacities:** bench coils are typically rigid 5-turn windings around 12–16 in. in diameter; **cable wraps** turn any part into its own coil — wrap heavy cable (sized for the amperage) in tight, counted turns and apply the same ampere-turn logic. Keep wraps snug: field strength falls off with distance from the conductor, so a loose wrap is a weak wrap.

sections.heading: Choosing, Evaluating, and Measuring Current — and Yokes
sections.body:
**Types of current required:** match the waveform to the expected flaw — AC for surface-breaking cracks (service-induced fatigue), HWDC or FWDC when near-subsurface discontinuities matter (manufacturing inspection of welds and castings). The calculated amperage is only a starting point; **current evaluation** proves it:

- **QQI artificial-flaw shims** taped flaw-side down on the actual part — the gold standard, proving strength *and* direction end-to-end.
- **Hall-effect gaussmeter** — procedures that specify a measured value commonly target a **tangential surface field on the order of 30–60 G** during the shot.
- **Pie (octagon) gauge** — fast qualitative directional check only.

**Measuring current:** the unit's ammeter is the legal record of the shot. Meters read differently by waveform (RMS for AC, average for rectified current), so follow the procedure's metering basis, keep the meter in calibration, and watch for drift — worn cables, loose connections, and tired rectifiers all show up as amperage you set but never delivered.

**Yokes** are portable electromagnets: flux runs from pole to pole through the part, so they detect flaws roughly **perpendicular to the line between the legs** — rotate 90° for full coverage. Performance is verified by **lifting power: about 10 lb for AC yokes, about 40 lb for DC yokes**, checked at the pole spacing used.

**Discontinuities commonly detected (longitudinal):** transverse and circumferential cracks — the fatigue cracks that grow across shafts, spindles, and axles in service.

sections.caption: Technique selection summary — field direction, typical current values, and the flaws each setup finds.
rows: Longitudinal seams, laps, cracks parallel to the part axis
Based on part diameter; offset: ≈ 4× conductor dia. coverage, ≈ 10% overlap
ID and OD flaws parallel to the axis
Circular, around the current path between tips
≈ 90–125 A per inch of spacing; spacing 6–8 in.
Flaws parallel to the prod line (welds, castings)
Lift check ≈ 10 lb AC / ≈ 40 lb DC
Cracks perpendicular to the pole-to-pole line

figures.title: Prod technique field and detectable flaw orientation

interactive.title: Plan the Shots: One Part, Full Coverage
interactive.intro: A hollow steel cylinder — 24 in. long, 3 in. OD, 2 in. bore — arrives with a welded mounting lug. The customer wants full MT coverage: ID corrosion-fatigue cracks running along the bore, possible transverse cracks on the body, and weld-toe cracks at the lug. Plan the work.

steps.prompt: First target: cracks running lengthwise along the bore (ID). Which setup magnetizes the ID for these flaws?
options: Head shot — clamp the cylinder between the headstocks
Central conductor through the bore
Coil shot at high amperage
steps.feedback: A head shot leaves the bore of a hollow part with almost no field, and a coil's longitudinal flux runs parallel to these flaws. A central conductor puts a circular field right at the ID — exactly what lengthwise bore cracks need.

steps.prompt: Your central conductor is a 1.5 in. copper bar laid against the bore wall (offset). How much ID circumference can you trust per shot?
options: The full circumference in one shot
An arc of about 6 in. — roughly 4× the conductor diameter — rotating the part with about 10% overlap between shots
Only the line of contact itself
steps.feedback: An offset central conductor reliably covers about 4 times its own diameter of circumference per shot: 4 × 1.5 in. = 6 in. Rotate and overlap about 10% until the full bore is covered.

steps.prompt: Next: transverse cracks on the 24 in. long, 3 in. OD body. You will use the 5-turn coil, low fill factor. What current do you expect, and how do you handle the part length?
options: About 1,125 A, magnetizing in roughly 18 in. sections with overlap
About 5,625 A in a single shot
About 360 A, full length in one shot
steps.feedback: L/D = 24 ÷ 3 = 8, so NI ≈ 45,000 ÷ 8 = 5,625 ampere-turns; through 5 turns that is about 1,125 A. A coil's effective zone is limited, so a 24 in. part gets shot in about 18 in. sections with overlap.

steps.prompt: The lug weld needs inspection for toe cracks, but the cylinder body is finish-machined and the customer prohibits arc strikes. Best tool?
options: Prods at 800 A — fastest option
An AC yoke, lift-verified at about 10 lb, worked in two orientations 90° apart
Skip the weld; the coil shot covered it
steps.feedback: Prods risk arc strikes and are banned on many finished surfaces. A yoke applies flux with no current through the part. Verify AC yoke lifting power at about 10 lb at the spacing used, and rotate 90° for both flaw directions.

steps.prompt: Before running the batch, how do you prove the technique — not just the arithmetic — is adequate?
options: The formulas were followed, so the technique is proven
Tape a QQI shim to the part at a representative location and confirm a clear indication; where the procedure requires a measured field, verify roughly 30–60 G tangential with a gaussmeter
Run one production part and assume the rest follow
steps.feedback: Calculations are starting points. The QQI proves field strength and direction on the real geometry, and the gaussmeter documents the tangential field where a measured value is required. That record defends every shot that follows.

checkQuestions.question: Prods are set 7 in. apart on a weldment and the procedure specifies 100 A per inch of prod spacing. What current should the shot deliver?
checkQuestions.explanation: Prod amperage is figured per inch of spacing: 7 in. × 100 A/in. = 700 A. Verify on the ammeter — contact quality and cable condition decide what is actually delivered.

checkQuestions.question: A head shot passes current lengthwise through a solid shaft. Which discontinuities does this setup detect?
options: Transverse fatigue cracks across the shaft
Seams and cracks running parallel to the shaft axis
Only subsurface inclusions
All orientations equally
checkQuestions.explanation: Current along the shaft makes flux circle the shaft; that circular flux crosses longitudinal flaws at 90 degrees, so seams and axial cracks are detected — transverse cracks need a coil shot instead.

checkQuestions.question: Why must prod or headstock contact never be broken while magnetizing current is flowing?
options: It demagnetizes the part instantly
Interrupting the circuit under load draws an arc that can burn the part — an arc strike is a hard, cracked, rejectable defect and an injury hazard
It damages the ammeter
It reverses the field direction
checkQuestions.explanation: Breaking a high-amperage circuit at the contact point draws an arc; arc strikes create local hard zones and cracks that can condemn the part, and the flash can injure the operator.

checkQuestions.question: Current flows around the turns of a magnetizing coil. Using the right-hand rule, the flux in a part inside the coil runs:
options: In circles around the part's own axis
Along the coil and part axis — perpendicular to the current in the turns
Radially outward from the centerline
Opposite to the current direction in the turns
checkQuestions.explanation: Flux is always perpendicular to the current that makes it: current circling the part in coil turns drives flux straight down the coil axis, producing longitudinal magnetization that detects transverse flaws.

checkQuestions.question: How is a yoke's adequacy commonly verified before use?
options: By measuring its coil resistance
By a lifting-power check — about 10 lb for AC yokes or about 40 lb for DC yokes, at the pole spacing to be used
By a 30-minute settling test
By checking that it holds a pie gauge to the part
checkQuestions.explanation: Standard practice verifies yokes by dead-weight lift: roughly 10 lb for AC and roughly 40 lb for DC at the working pole spacing, per the governing procedure.

references: CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §2.5 (magnetization by means of electric current: circular and longitudinal techniques, prods and plates, coils and cables, current determination and measurement, yokes)
CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §3.1.1.3–3.1.1.4 (field in parts through which current flows; methods of inducing current flow)
Practices such as ASTM E709, ASTM E1444, and ASME Section V Article 7 give technique and amperage guidance of this form (generic reference)
Reference values: 300–800 A/in. diameter (circular); 90–125 A/in. spacing (prods); NI ≈ 45,000/(L/D) (coil); 10 lb AC / 40 lb DC yoke lift; 30–60 G tangential field when measured
